channel.title: NYT > Business
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Oil Rises Slightly After Trump’s Latest Threats on Iran
item.description: President Trump taunted Iranian leaders on Sunday, threatening to escalate attacks if the Strait of Hormuz was not reopened.
item.dc:creator: Emmett Lindner
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: This Is Not China’s War, but Beijing Started Preparing for It Years Ago
item.description: Long concerned about geopolitical crises, China redoubled efforts to secure energy security when President Trump started raising the stakes in his first term.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Stevenson and Murphy Zhao
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Oil storage tanks and facilities of a Sinopec plant in Shanghai last month.

item.title: China Mourned an Education Influencer. The Grief Was a Quiet Revolt.
item.description: Zhang Xuefeng helped people navigate the country’s unforgiving higher education system. The public outpouring after his death was a quiet rebuke to the punishing process.
item.dc:creator: Li Yuan
item.media:description: Mourners lined up in Suzhou, China, to pay their respects to Zhang Xuefeng, a prominent education influencer who died on March 24.

item.title: OPEC Plus Warns of Slow Recovery After War in Iran
item.description: The group of influential oil exporters also said it was increasing its production quotas, a largely symbolic move.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Mullin and Rebecca F. Elliott
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries
item.media:description: Smoke rising from an oil warehouse following a suspected drone attack last week near Erbil, Iraq.

item.title: Despite Poor Reviews, ‘The Super Mario Galaxy Movie’ Is a Megahit
item.description: The animated sequel collected about $373 million worldwide over its first five days in theaters. “Kids love the movie,” a box office analyst said.
item.dc:creator: Brooks Barnes
item.media:description: “The Super Mario Galaxy Movie,” based on Nintendo characters, cost roughly $110 million to make.

item.title: New Chef Program Helps People With Autism Find Jobs in Fine-Dining Restaurants
item.description: Culinary jobs have the potential to be a perfect fit, and a new effort is afoot to help autistic workers land them.
item.dc:creator: Pete Wells
item.media:description: Joseph Valentino, left, a cook at Point Seven in Manhattan, and Franklin Becker, the restaurant’s owner. Mr. Valentino, who has autism, helped inspired a new program to place people on the autism spectrum in fine-dining jobs.

item.title: Hockey Rinks Turn to Plastic Ice as Planet Warms
item.description: Some environmentalists question using plastic to address rising temperatures.
item.dc:creator: Ken Belson and Hiroko Tabuchi
item.media:credit: Rebecca Gratz for The New York Times
item.media:description: Climate change has wreaked havoc with many sports. But few sports have been affected as much as those played on ice and snow.

item.title: New Owner of Shopping Plaza in Kansas City Pitches a $1.5 Billion Rescue Plan
item.description: The Country Club Plaza’s sale and turnaround plans come at a consequential economic time for Kansas City, Mo., which will soon lose the Chiefs football team.
item.dc:creator: Joe Gose

item.title: Oil Markets Doubt Trump’s Peace Signals as Tensions With Iran Grow
item.description: After seesawing between diplomacy and threats, the president may find it harder to knock down prices by suggesting pauses in attacks or progress in talks.
item.dc:creator: Amy Fan
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Hollywood Writers Union and Studios Reach Contract Deal
item.description: With the global entertainment business reeling during a period of rapid change, there was little enthusiasm on either side for a costly standoff.
item.dc:creator: Brooks Barnes
item.category: Alliance of Motion Picture and Television Producers
item.media:credit: Jenna Schoenefeld for The New York Times
item.media:description: Members of the Writers Guild of America on striking in 2023. The contract talks then were particularly drawn out and acrimonious.

item.title: Orion Samuelson, the Elvis Presley of Agricultural Radio, Dies at 91
item.description: As a host at WGN in Chicago for 60 years, he shared news that was essential to farmers in a homespun style that appealed to listeners from the city.
item.dc:creator: Michael S. Rosenwald

item.title: Trump Slashed Science Funding. Now the U.S. Could Face a Costly Brain Drain.
item.description: The White House’s attacks on academia and budget cuts for research have provided an opening for other countries to poach leading scientists.
item.dc:creator: Vivienne Walt
item.media:credit: David Payr for The New York Times
item.media:description: Wali Malik, a robotics engineer, left his job in Massachusetts last year to lead a new lab in Vienna.

item.title: My Husband Can’t Get a Job. Should I Divorce Him?
item.description: Plus, whether to use A.I. to get ahead at work — if you think the technology is evil.
item.dc:creator: Max Read
item.media:credit: Photo illustration by Margeaux Walter for The New York Times

item.title: Trump Budget Does Little to Address Nation’s Fiscal Challenges
item.description: While the federal deficit has started to shrink under President Trump, his plans could make the long-term situation worse.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Duehren and Alan Rappeport
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump’s latest budget proposal includes little that would realistically bolster the country’s financial health.

item.title: Some Borrowers Are Moving Abroad and Abandoning Their Student Loans
item.description: A record number of student loan borrowers are in delinquency and default. Some are making the drastic decision to leave the country and abandon their loans.
item.dc:creator: Laura O’Connor
item.media:credit: Milan Bures for The New York Times
item.media:description: Amanda Lynn Tully, 37, moved to Prague and defaulted on her student loans. She hasn’t made a payment in over seven years.

item.title: Uber and Lyft Offer Gas Price Relief, but Drivers Say It’s Not Enough
item.description: As fuel costs go up, making a living as a gig driver is harder than ever.
item.dc:creator: Natallie Rocha and Ricardo Nagaoka
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: ‘I Got Back Every Penny’: Inside Trump’s Supercharged Tax Season
item.description: The law Republicans passed last year has so far been largely imperceptible to most Americans. That’s changing as tens of millions file their taxes this spring.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Duehren
item.media:credit: Justin T. Gellerson for The New York Times, left; Jamie Kelter Davis for The New York Times

item.title: In the Philippines, Rising Fuel Prices Force Travelers to Stay Home During Holy Week
item.description: Surging gasoline prices in the Philippines have forced some people to cancel or scale back the Visita Iglesia, a Holy Week tradition in which Catholics travel to seven churches.
item.dc:creator: Francesca Regalado and Ezra Acayan
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Filipinos heading to the provinces for the Holy Week lining up at a bus terminal in Quezon City, Philippines, on Thursday.

item.title: ‘Under Protest,’ Raw Dairy Farm Recalls Cheddar Linked to 9 E. Coli Cases
item.description: Raw Farm, a dairy producer in California, denies that its products have caused the outbreak, which has sickened people in three states.
item.dc:creator: Alice Callahan
item.media:description: Raw Farm shredded Cheddar cheese on display at a grocery store in Los Angeles in 2023.

item.title: United to Offer No-Frills Fares in Its Premium Cabins
item.description: United is introducing restrictive base fares in its most profitable cabins: premium economy and business class.
item.dc:creator: Niraj Chokshi
item.media:description: United Airlines is the industry’s second-most profitable company, in large part because of its premium offerings.

item.title: Big Banks Seeking a Piece of SpaceX’s I.P.O. Must Subscribe to Elon Musk’s Grok
item.description: Mr. Musk is requiring Wall Street firms to purchase subscriptions to his A.I. chatbot if they want to advise on one of the largest initial public offerings in history.
item.dc:creator: Maureen Farrell
item.media:description: Elon Musk has promoted Grok, his A.I. chatbot, as the antidote to political correctness.

item.title: Why Is the Labor Market Stuck?
item.description: Our chief economics correspondent, Ben Casselman, describes how a “low-hire, low-fire” labor market has left American job-seekers in a bind.
item.dc:creator: Ben Casselman, Sutton Raphael, June Kim, Nour Idriss and Christina Djossa

item.title: What to know about the report.
item.dc:creator: Lydia DePillis

item.title: Parents Who Borrowed for Children’s College Face Looming Deadline
item.description: New rules mean that parent PLUS loans have to be consolidated into a new loan by June 30 for parents to keep affordable payments. But the deadline is really earlier to allow time for processing.
item.dc:creator: Ann Carrns

item.title: Trump Requests $1.5 Trillion for Military Spending
item.description: The huge proposed increase would be partly offset by steep cuts to domestic programs, some of which the Trump administration describes as wasteful.
item.dc:creator: Tony Romm
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump has framed his proposed budget increase for defense in urgent terms.

item.title: Strong Jobs Numbers Make the Fed’s Job Easier
item.description: Robust job growth in March suggests that the labor market remains relatively healthy, allowing officials at the central bank to focus on fighting inflation.
item.dc:creator: Ben Casselman and Colby Smith
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Caroline Gutman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Jerome H. Powell, the Federal Reserve chair, speaking at an event this week, did not convey any immediate urgency to take action.

item.title: The White House is readying a budget for the statistics agency that compiles the jobs report.
item.description: President Trump is set to release his new spending plan on Friday, after trying last year to cut funding for the federal bureau tasked with measuring the economy.
item.dc:creator: Tony Romm
item.media:description: The Bureau of Labor Statistics would struggle under the sort of budget cut that President Trump has proposed in the past.

item.title: March Jobs Report Shows Stronger U.S. Market Than Expected With 178,000 New Positions
item.description: Payrolls expanded and unemployment dropped last month after a health care strike ended and a harsh winter abated.
item.dc:creator: Lydia DePillis

item.title: Economists Are Drawing Stronger Connections Between A.I. and Jobs
item.description: Artificial intelligence hasn’t disrupted the labor market, economists say, but they are increasingly convinced that it will — and that policymakers are unprepared.
item.dc:creator: Ben Casselman

item.title: Skilled Foreign Workers Think About Leaving the U.S.
item.description: As the Trump administration cracks down on the H-1B visa program, which allows skilled workers like software engineers to work in the United States, foreign professionals are debating whether to stay and build careers or quit the American Dream.
item.dc:creator: Nailah Morgan, Jon Hazell and Rafaela Balster

item.title: Jobs and Workers Are In Balance. Nobody Is Happy About It.
item.description: Lower immigration has brought labor supply in line with shaky demand, but economists worry that such a slow-moving job market is at risk of toppling over.
item.dc:creator: Lydia DePillis

item.title: How New Yorkers Spend, Splurge and Scrimp to Live in the City
item.description: Eliza Shapiro, who reports on New York City’s affordability crisis, asked hundreds of residents to get candid about their finances.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Bahr
item.media:credit: Anna Watts for The New York Times

item.title: Rival Nations Seize On Choke Points to Counter Trump
item.description: From Iran to China, President Trump’s global aggression has encouraged other countries to search for new ways to pressure the U.S. economy.
item.dc:creator: Ana Swanson
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump has claimed that his international aggression has only upsides.

item.title: United Raises Bag Fees, Following JetBlue’s Lead as Fuel Costs Soar
item.description: As the war in Iran strains U.S. airlines, JetBlue and United are now charging up to $10 more for checked bags on domestic flights. More carriers could follow.
item.dc:creator: Christine Chung
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: JetBlue Airways this week became one of the first U.S. carriers to raise bag fees since the war in Iran began, blaming rising operating costs for the move. United Airlines raised its fees days later.

item.title: Hershey’s Promises to Use Only Real Chocolate After Backlash
item.description: The change follows weeks of complaints from a grandson of the Reese’s Peanut Butter Cup inventor and other vocal consumers.
item.dc:creator: Alice Callahan
item.media:description: The Hershey Company said it would return to using “classic milk and dark chocolate recipes” in all of its products by 2027, adding that it was responding to consumer preferences.

item.title: Trump Revamps Metal and Pharmaceutical Tariffs
item.description: The administration announced new levies and made changes to existing tariffs for industries that have proved influential on the president’s trade policy.
item.dc:creator: Ana Swanson and Rebecca Robbins
item.media:description: A pharmacy in Los Angeles. For the Trump administration, the threat of pharmaceutical tariffs has acted as a lever to bring companies to the negotiating table.

item.title: Republicans Seek Protections for Oil Giants Against Climate Lawsuits
item.description: Utah passed a law to insulate oil companies against efforts to make them pay for their role in global warming. A nationwide version is in the works.
item.dc:creator: David Gelles and Karen Zraick
item.media:credit: Micah Green for The New York Times
item.media:description: Exxon, Chevron and ConocoPhillips face potentially costly legal claims over the role of fossil fuels in global warming. A Chevron facility in Mississippi.

item.title: War With Iran Clarifies Trump’s Spending Priorities: The Military, Not Child Care
item.description: As the White House prepares to release its 2027 budget, President Trump said military protection, not social programs, took precedence.
item.dc:creator: Alan Rappeport
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: A building in Tehran damaged by airstrikes. This week, President Trump said: “It’s not possible for us to take care of day care, Medicaid, Medicare, all of these individual things, they can do it on a state basis.”

item.title: Blue Owl, Ailing Private Credit Firm, Reveals Even More Troubles
item.description: Investors in the struggling Wall Street lender put in record requests to get their money back.
item.dc:creator: Rob Copeland
item.media:description: Marc Lipschultz, co-chief executive of Blue Owl Capital. Blue Owl’s executives, in letters to backers on Thursday, blamed a litany of factors for its troubles.

item.title: Mortgage Rates Climb for 5th Week as Iran War Weighs on U.S. Housing Market
item.description: The average rate on a 30-year mortgage in the United States jumped to 6.46 percent, making it harder for buyers to afford homes.
item.dc:creator: Gregory Schmidt
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Standard & Poor's Case-Shiller Home Price Index

item.title: Trump’s Media-Bashing Is Coming Back to Bite Him in Court
item.description: Judges have cited attacks on the press by the president and his appointees when ruling against the government in at least three court cases.
item.dc:creator: Erik Wemple
item.media:description: President Trump in the Oval Office on Tuesday.

item.title: Trump Faces a Tough Fight With His New Budget
item.description: Last year, the president proposed many steep spending cuts that Congress never granted. This time, he may face an even tougher sell.
item.dc:creator: Tony Romm
item.media:description: President Trump is likely to ask lawmakers to pour new money into the military.

item.title: Investigators Said to Look at Whether LaGuardia Controller Stepped Away
item.description: Officials are said to be examining whether a controller had to use an emergency phone just before the crash at LaGuardia. They have yet to determine the cause of the crash and are also focusing on other avenues.
item.dc:creator: Kate Kelly and Karoun Demirjian
item.media:credit: Dave Sanders for The New York Times
item.media:description: Investigators are said to be examining whether an air traffic controller stepped away from his console in a critical moment before the deadly accident at LaGuardia Airport on March 22.

item.title: What the Iran War Could Mean for Stocks, Bonds and Growth
item.description: A merely bad outlook might be good enough for the markets, our columnist says.
item.dc:creator: Jeff Sommer
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Tesla Sales Rise as $4-a-Gallon Gas Revives Interest in E.V.s
item.description: The repeal of tax credits last year sent electric vehicle sales plummeting. But with gas prices soaring, consumers are taking another, cautious, look.
item.dc:creator: Jack Ewing
item.category: Cadillac Division of General Motors Corp
item.media:credit: Rachel Bujalski for The New York Times
item.media:description: A Tesla dealership in Corte Madera, Calif.

item.title: Why Stocks Tumbled After Trump’s Iran Address
item.description: Oil surged and stocks sold off after the president delivered an unclear case for continuing the war in the Middle East.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly and Brian O’Keefe
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Investors were largely unimpressed with President Trump’s address on the war in Iran.

item.title: Europe Pushes for a Gentler Internet for Children
item.description: The European Union and national capitals are trying to make social media and algorithms less addictive and safer, especially for children.
item.dc:creator: Jeanna Smialek
item.media:credit: Andrea Mantovani for The New York Times
item.media:description: Stéphanie Mistre, whose daughter, Marie Le Tiec, ended her life in 2021 at age 15, has become an activist for children’s online safety.

item.title: The Revival of the Fashion-Tech Love Affair
item.description: Get ready for the return of wearables and a new stage in the fashion-tech relationship.
item.dc:creator: Vanessa Friedman
item.media:credit: Adrienne Grunwald for The New York Times
item.media:description: At the new Meta Lab flagship on Fifth Avenue in Manhattan.

item.title: How A.I. Helped One Man (and His Brother) Build a $1.8 Billion Company
item.description: Who needs more than two employees when artificial intelligence can do so many corporate tasks? It’s super efficient — and a little bit lonely.
item.dc:creator: Erin Griffith
item.media:credit: Maggie Shannon for The New York Times
item.media:description: Matthew Gallagher, 41, built his start-up, Medvi, with artificial intelligence and few humans.
